L1.area: Authentication and authorization extensions and middleware

L2.description: A cryptographic weakness exists where a non-cryptographically-secure pseudo-random number generator is used to generate a fallback secret for signing authentication cookies when the relevant security configuration property is not explicitly set. An attacker could predict or brute-force the generated secret, potentially enabling token forgery or authentication bypass. Additionally, each process generates its own independent fallback secret, leading to inconsistent authentication state across distributed deployments.

L3.area: Kerberos authentication extension and cookie signature handling
L3.description: A cryptographic weakness exists where a non-cryptographically-secure pseudo-random number generator is used to generate a fallback secret for signing Kerberos authentication cookies when the cookie signing configuration is not explicitly provided. An attacker could predict or brute-force the generated secret, enabling token forgery or authentication bypass. Each process generates its own independent fallback secret, causing inconsistent authentication state across distributed multi-broker deployments.

ground_truth.reason: The Kerberos authenticator uses ThreadLocalRandom (a non-cryptographically-secure PRNG) to generate a fallback cookie signature secret when the configuration is not explicitly set, allowing an attacker to predict or brute-force the secret and forge authentication tokens.
